title: Risotto al gorgonzola
subtitle: Creamy blue cheese risotto with toasted walnuts
excerpt: Gorgonzola melted through a risotto at the end of cooking, taking the place of the parmesan in the final emulsion. Richer and more pungent than a standard risotto, balanced by bitter walnuts. Short, straightforward, and a reliable dinner party dish when you want something that looks more involved than it is.
sourceNotes: Risotto al gorgonzola is a Lombard preparation, given that Gorgonzola is a Lombard DOP cheese produced near Milan. The pairing of blue cheese with risotto is documented in Milanese cooking from the late nineteenth century. Artusi includes Lombard cheese preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain). Gorgonzola dolce (young, mild) is preferred over piccante (aged, sharp) for this dish.

glossaryTerms.definition: The final step of risotto-making: cold butter and grated cheese beaten vigorously into the hot rice off the heat. The rapid agitation emulsifies the fat with the starchy cooking liquid, creating the characteristic creamy, flowing texture of a properly made risotto.

content.text: Gorgonzola dolce has a soft, almost spreadable texture and a mild, creamy funk that intensifies when melted into a risotto. It does not taste like a cheese course dropped into a rice dish. It tastes like a very good, slightly unusual risotto, and people who cannot identify it often ask what gives it that extra depth.

Use gorgonzola dolce rather than piccante. The aged variety is too sharp and becomes grainy when heated. Dolce melts smoothly and integrates into the rice without curdling. Toast the walnuts in a dry pan — it takes two minutes and makes a meaningful difference to both flavour and texture.

items.prepNote: 20 g for cooking, 20 g cold for mantecatura
taste before adding; gorgonzola is salty

content.text: Put the {{walnuts}} of walnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat. Toast for 2 minutes, shaking the pan, until fragrant and slightly darkened. Tip onto a board and break roughly. Set aside.

Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and 20 g of the {{unsalted-butter}} in a wide, heavy pan over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and cook for 5 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the {{arborio-rice}} of risotto rice and stir for 2 minutes until the grains look translucent at the edges.

Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine. Stir until absorbed. Add the hot {{stock-vegetable}} a ladleful at a time, stirring constantly and waiting for each addition to absorb before adding the next. Cook for 16 to 18 minutes until the rice is creamy and just cooked through.
Remove from the heat. Add the remaining cold butter in pieces and the {{gorgonzola}} of gorgonzola, broken into chunks. Beat vigorously for 60 seconds: this is the

. The gorgonzola should melt completely into the rice, making it glossy and flowing. Grind over a generous amount of black pepper. Taste for salt. Cover and rest for 2 minutes. Serve in warm bowls, topped with the toasted walnuts.

items.cause: Piccante (aged) gorgonzola used, or heat still on when cheese was added
items.fix: Use gorgonzola dolce only; add the cheese off the heat; it should melt smoothly into the risotto

items.cause: Gorgonzola is already very salty; salt added without tasting first
items.fix: Always taste before seasoning; with gorgonzola, you may need no additional salt at all

items.symptom: Blue cheese flavour is too strong
items.cause: Too much gorgonzola, or piccante used
items.fix: Start with 100 g and add more to taste; use dolce rather than piccante
Use fresh walnuts within their best-before date; toast until just fragrant, not dark

content.text: Gorgonzola is one of the oldest named cheeses in Italian cooking, documented from at least the ninth century and associated with the town of Gorgonzola, east of Milan. Like Parmesan, it is a DOP product with a defined production area and method. The pairing with risotto is natural in Lombardy, where both rice and gorgonzola are locally produced. The dish is quiet about its origin: served without announcement it reads as a rich, slightly unusual risotto rather than a blue-cheese statement. That understatement is part of what makes it work at a table where some guests are uncertain about strong cheese.
